subject: Science
chapter: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

key_points: The eye lens adjusts its curvature to maintain a clear image on the retina
Accommodation allows the eye to focus on objects at varying distances
Vision defects occur due to abnormal shape of the eyeball or improper focal length of the eye lens
Myopia occurs when the eyeball becomes elongated or the eye lens becomes too powerful
Hypermetropia occurs when the eyeball becomes too short or the eye lens has insufficient converging power
Presbyopia results from ageing which reduces the flexibility of the eye lens and weakens ciliary muscles
Atmospheric refraction occurs due to variation in the refractive index of different layers of air
examples: Concave lenses diverge light rays and help correct myopia
Convex lenses converge light rays and help correct hypermetropia
Bifocal lenses are used to correct presbyopia
Stars twinkle because their light continuously refracts through different atmospheric layers
Planets do not twinkle because they appear as extended sources of light
The Sun appears flattened at sunrise and sunset due to atmospheric refraction
formulas: Near point → Minimum distance for clear vision (25 cm for normal eye)
Far point → Maximum distance for clear vision (infinity for normal eye)
Dispersion → Splitting of white light into component colours due to different refractive indices
Scattering → Deviation of light in different directions by particles in a medium
Tyndall effect → Scattering of light by colloidal particles making the path of light visible
exam_tips: Clearly understand the mechanism of accommodation in the human eye
Practice ray diagrams for correction of myopia and hypermetropia
Learn the causes of atmospheric phenomena such as twinkling of stars and red sunset
Revise the concept of scattering and Tyndall effect carefully
Focus on conceptual questions about atmospheric refraction and vision defects
